region_of_origin: Indonesia and Malay Archipelago; widely adopted across Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn URI exists for sambal oelek specifically. FOODON_03301710 (chili sauce) is the closest defensible parent class. Gap noted.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to sambal oelek. A0FJP (chilli sauce) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of sambal oelek is 1 teaspoon (approximately 6g), which delivers approximately 2 kcal, 0.5g carbohydrate, and 36mg sodium — making it one of the lowest-impact condiment additions in the Singaporean pantry.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Oelek — USDA Branded Foods (Huy Fong Foods) and USDA FoodData Central raw red chilli reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central — Branded Foods and SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA Branded Foods entry for Huy Fong Sambal Oelek and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy raw red chilli pepper data. Values represent the commercial Huy Fong product as the dominant Singapore market reference, with homemade equivalents being nutritionally comparable. Fat is confirmed 0.0g per USDA Branded Foods label data for this product formulation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chilli-based condiments — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database raw chilli and low-carbohydrate condiment references
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal oelek in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on: (1) raw red chilli pepper GI references in the University of Sydney database indicating low GI for chilli-based foods; (2) the product's low carbohydrate content (~8g/100g, predominantly from chilli cell-wall carbohydrates and residual sugars); (3) the presence of capsaicin, which has documented effects on slowing gastric emptying and reducing postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 6g teaspoon serving = (30 × 0.48g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.14 — negligible. This is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with approved GI source methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium per serving (36mg per teaspoon); capsaicin has mild vasodilatory properties
hypertension.guidance: At the standard 1-teaspoon (6g) serving, sambal oelek contributes only 36mg sodium — well within safe limits for hypertensive patients — and capsaicin in chilli has demonstrated mild vasodilatory and blood-pressure-lowering effects at regular dietary consumption, making this one of the most hypertension-friendly condiment choices available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycaemic load (GL ~0.14 per teaspoon); capsaicin reduces postprandial glucose and may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal oelek has a negligible glycaemic load of approximately 0.14 per teaspoon serving, and multiple RCTs have demonstrated that dietary capsaicin reduces postprandial blood glucose, slows gastric emptying, and may modestly improve insulin sensitivity — making it a clinically appropriate and potentially beneficial condiment choice for patients with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, very low calorie density; capsaicin has anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal oelek contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and negligible calories per serving, and capsaicin has been associated in observational and mechanistic studies with reduced LDL oxidation, anti-inflammatory effects, and modest improvements in lipid profiles — supporting its use as a cardiovascular-safe condiment when used in standard teaspoon quantities.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsaicin TRPV1 receptor desensitisation; anti-nociceptive and anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dietary capsaicin from sambal oelek contributes to systemic capsaicin levels that may exert modest anti-nociceptive effects via TRPV1 receptor desensitisation, consistent with the well-documented analgesic evidence base for topical capsaicin in rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal oelek is a raw ground chilli paste condiment of Indonesian and Malay origin, consisting of minimally processed red chillies ground with salt and a small amount of vinegar or lime juice — the simplest and most nutritionally pure form of sambal. The name derives from the Malay and Indonesian word 'oelek' (also spelled 'ulek'), referring to the mortar-and-pestle grinding action used in traditional preparation. In Singapore, the dominant commercial version is produced by Huy Fong Foods (the Vietnamese-American brand famous for its rooster logo sriracha), though homemade versions using fresh red chillies are nutritionally equivalent. Sambal oelek has a bright, clean chilli heat with a slightly acidic note from the vinegar, and a coarse, paste-like texture. It is used as a table condiment, dipping sauce, and cooking base across Singaporean, Indonesian, Malaysian, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines. Nutritionally, sambal oelek is one of the most benign condiments in the Singaporean pantry: per 100g it provides only 35 kcal, 1.5g protein, 0g fat, 8g carbohydrate, and 600mg sodium — but the critical clinical point is that the standard serving is just 1 teaspoon (6g), delivering approximately 2 kcal, 0g fat, 0.5g carbohydrate, and only 36mg sodium. The active compound capsaicin has the strongest evidence base of any naturally occurring food compound for analgesic effects (via TRPV1 receptor desensitisation), and multiple RCTs demonstrate modest postprandial glucose reduction, slowed gastric emptying, and thermogenic effects. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, sambal oelek is a clinically appropriate condiment choice. The only relevant clinical consideration is individual heat and spice tolerance.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA Branded Foods (Huy Fong Sambal Oelek) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy raw red chilli data — both approved primary sources. The Huy Fong product is the dominant commercial reference in Singapore. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present. All hard gates pass.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero by USDA Branded Foods label data for the Huy Fong Sambal Oelek product formulation — this product contains no added oil or fat; the chilli, salt, and vinegar formulation is inherently fat-free. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a vegan plant-based condiment. Vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based condiment with no fortification. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for sambal oelek not available in USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry as of 2026; the total fat is 0.0g so these values are effectively zero but cannot be independently confirmed from the source. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the USDA Branded Foods entry for this product. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database chilli and low-carbohydrate condiment references; no direct GI measurement for sambal oelek exists in the database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 30 × available carbohydrate 0.48g per 6g serving) / 100 = 0.14. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 is the closest parent class (chili sauce); no dedicated sambal oelek URI exists. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (chilli sauce) is the closest defensible matrix code.
